lead: Most moving-day disasters are not bad luck; they are avoidable mistakes made weeks earlier. After twenty years of moving Suffolk families, we have seen the same handful of slip-ups trip people up again and again, and almost every one is easy to sidestep once you know it is coming. The reassuring part is that avoiding these mistakes costs nothing but a little forethought. Here are the most common moving mistakes we see, and exactly how to make sure they do not happen to you.

sections.h: Leaving Everything Until the Last Minute
sections.body:
By far the most common mistake is underestimating how long a move takes and starting too late. Packing a whole house, sorting the admin and decluttering all take far more time than people expect, and cramming it into the final few days turns a manageable move into a frantic one. Rushing is also when things get damaged, forgotten or done badly, so the time you save by delaying tends to cost you dearly later.

The fix is simply to start early and spread the effort. The moment you know you are moving, begin thinking about it, even before a date is fixed. Make a rough plan, work backwards from your likely moving day, and tackle a little each week rather than facing the whole house at once. A move done calmly over several weeks is a completely different experience from one thrown together in a panic, and it shows in the result.

Starting early also gives you options that vanish under time pressure. You can declutter properly, gather good materials, get accurate quotes, and choose a sensible moving date rather than taking whatever is left. In our experience, the single biggest predictor of a smooth move is not the size of the house or the distance; it is how early the family began. Time is free, and it is the most valuable resource you have.

A close second is moving everything, including the things you no longer want. Because removals cost is driven by volume, packing and transporting belongings you will only put straight into another cupboard is wasted effort and wasted money. People often pack on autopilot, boxing up the loft and the back of the wardrobe without ever asking whether those things deserve a place in the new home at all.

Worse still is decluttering at the wrong time. Many people have a clear-out after they have been quoted, or even as they pack, which means they did the work too late to benefit. The quote was built on a full house, so the price does not drop. Declutter before your survey, go through every room, the loft, the garage and the shed, and decide honestly what to keep, sell, donate or recycle. Then quote, then pack, in that order.

Poor packing is a mistake that punishes you twice, once in breakages and again in the chaos of unpacking. Overfilled boxes that are too heavy to lift safely, fragile items wrapped carelessly or not at all, and boxes with no label all cause problems on the day and afterwards. The few minutes saved by skimping on packing are lost many times over when something shatters or you cannot find anything in your new home.

Pack properly instead. Use sturdy boxes, keep weights manageable by putting heavy items in small boxes, wrap fragile things individually with plenty of cushioning, and never leave a box only half full and rattling. Above all, label every box with its room and a note of the contents, because that single habit transforms the unpacking and tells the crew what needs care. Good packing is slower up front and far faster overall.

Know your limits, too. The kitchen glassware, delicate electricals, mirrors and wardrobes are where damage most often happens, and these are well worth leaving to professional packers even if you do the rest yourself. Trying to do everything to save money, then packing the fragile items badly under time pressure, is a false economy. A sensible split, easy rooms yourself and the awkward items to the crew, avoids the worst of it.

sections.h: Forgetting the Essentials Box and the Admin
sections.body:
A surprisingly common and miserable mistake is loading absolutely everything onto the lorry, including the things you need that very first night. Arriving exhausted at a new home and not being able to find the kettle, the bedding, the phone chargers or the children's things makes a hard day harder. The remedy is a simple essentials box, kept with you and not on the van, holding everything you will want immediately on arrival.

The other half of this mistake is neglecting the admin until it is too late. Utilities not arranged at either home, no meter readings taken, post not redirected, and addresses not updated with the bank, employer, doctor and council all cause headaches that follow you for weeks. None of it is difficult, but there is a lot of it, so work through it steadily in the weeks before the move rather than discovering it on the day or after.

Keep your valuables, important documents and anything irreplaceable with you personally, too. Passports, paperwork, jewellery and medication should travel with you, not in a box among hundreds. This is partly practical and partly peace of mind, and it avoids the sinking feeling of not knowing which of a hundred sealed boxes holds the one thing you suddenly need. A little planning here saves a great deal of stress.

sections.h: Choosing a Mover on Price Alone
sections.body:
One of the costliest mistakes is picking a removals firm purely on the lowest number. A tempting price from a casual operator can hide real risks: no proper protection, an inaccurate quote that swells on the day, or a crew with little experience of the things that go wrong. The few pounds saved up front can become a very expensive lesson if belongings are damaged and the loss falls entirely on you.

Avoid this by looking past the headline figure. Insist on a proper survey so the quote is accurate, get it in writing, and ask exactly what is included. Crucially, ask whether the move is fully covered and confirm that protection before you book; every move we carry out is fully covered, and any reputable firm will state this gladly. A clear, complete quote from an accountable firm is far better value than a vague low one.

Be wary, too, of comparing quotes that are not really comparable. A higher figure that includes materials, dismantling and proper protection may well be cheaper in truth than a lower one that quietly omits them. Make sure you are comparing like with like, and treat reluctance to answer straightforward questions as a warning sign. The right mover is the one who gives you an honest, complete picture, not simply the smallest number.

sections.h: Not Planning for Things to Go Slightly Wrong
sections.body:
The final mistake is assuming everything will run perfectly to plan, with no margin for the hiccups that moves so often throw up. Chains slip, keys arrive late, traffic snarls, and the weather does as it pleases. A plan with no slack in it falls apart the moment anything moves, whereas a plan with sensible breathing room absorbs a hitch and carries on. Expecting the odd bump is not pessimism; it is wisdom.

So build in a little flexibility, keep communicating, and work with a firm experienced enough to stay calm when something unexpected crops up. Avoid the common mistakes, start early, declutter, pack well, keep your essentials and admin in hand, and choose an accountable mover, and you remove the great majority of what can go wrong. If you would like a free, no-obligation quote and honest advice on getting your move right first time, we would be glad to help.

faqs: What is the most common moving mistake?

Starting too late. People underestimate how long packing, admin and decluttering take, then cram it all into a few frantic days. Rushing is when things get damaged or forgotten. Beginning early and spreading the effort over weeks is the single biggest predictor of a smooth move.
When should I declutter before a move?

Before your survey, not after. The quote is built on what we see, so clearing out belongings once you have been quoted does not lower the price. Declutter every room, the loft, the garage and the shed first, then get your quote, then pack, in that order.
Why shouldn't I just pick the cheapest mover?

A very cheap casual operator may carry no real protection or give an inaccurate quote that grows on the day, leaving any loss with you. That is a false economy. Insist on a survey, get it in writing, and confirm the move is fully covered before you book.
What should I keep with me rather than on the van?

An essentials box with the kettle, mugs, tea, chargers, toiletries and a change of clothes, plus all valuables, important documents, jewellery and medication. Keeping these with you avoids hunting through sealed boxes and gives genuine peace of mind on a busy day.
